original_abstract: Uncovering the atomic structure of a catalyst is fundamental to identifying the mechanisms driving its performance. Because catalysts rarely maintain their as-synthesized states under operating conditions, structural characterization is best conducted through in situ experimentation. In this study, we employ in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) to investigate Pt3Ni nanoframes, a class of materials recognized for their exceptional electrocatalytic activity toward the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR). By probing surface characteristics through electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition and carbon monoxide electrooxidation, we observed that surface structural variations directly influence the binding strength of adsorbate molecules. While it is established that the formation of a "Pt-skin" enhances ORR activity by weakening these binding energies, our XAS results provide deeper insight into this transition. We find that nanoframes with stronger adsorbate binding possess rougher surfaces resulting from inadequate Pt segregation and subsequent Ni dissolution. Conversely, nanoframes demonstrating superior ORR activity exhibit pronounced Pt segregation over Ni-rich subsurface layers, facilitating the formation of a high-quality Pt-skin. This work concludes that the remarkable catalytic performance of Pt3Ni hollow nanoframes is primarily dictated by the successful development of this protective and active Pt-skin architecture.
humanized_abstract: Understanding the atomic structure of catalysts is often key to understanding their activity and mechanism. Typically catalysts are not synthesised in the exact structure they are found in when operating. Therefore structural characterisation of catalysts is often conducted through in situ synthesis methods such as X-ray absorption spectroscopy. This is exactly what has been used by these researchers when investigating Pt3Ni nanoframes and their performance as catalysts for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), a commonly required catalytic reaction in numerous devices, including but not limited to; proton exchange membrane (PEM) fuel cells. In the structure shown in the accompanying illustration the Pt3Ni nanoframe acts as a scaffold that is supported on a carbon scaffold (not shown) and that the catalyst surface has been probed through electrochemical hydrogen underpotential deposition (HUPD) and carbon monoxide electrooxidation. In such systems such experiments can demonstrate how different surfaces have different stabilities as a result of differences in adsorbate bonding on the catalyst surface. Catalysts It has previously been shown through similar techniques that an enhanced "Pt-skin" surface is significantly improved when increased stability within the bond between the adsorbate molecule and the catalyst is decreased, leading to an enhancement of catalytic properties, specifically in the ORR activity of the material. Using in situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy it has been shown that those catalyst materials with the increased adsorbate binding have had less or no surface Pt segregation and subsequent Ni dissolution resulting in a higher surface roughness in comparison with their catalyst analogue having improved ORR activity due to further segregation of the Pt surface element above the Ni-rich catalytic centre resulting in the improved activity and desirable Pt-skin surface. Therefore it has been found and concluded by the researchers that the performance of these catalysts can be directly related to the generation of an active high surface area Pt-skin over layer of the material.

document.input: Uncovering the atomic structure of a catalyst is fundamental to identifying the mechanisms driving its performance. Because catalysts rarely maintain their as-synthesized states under operating conditions, structural characterization is best conducted through in situ experimentation.
